---
title: "Working with Data in R"
subtitle: "DMgtAsia · Session-III"
format:
  live-html:
    toc: true
  live-revealjs:
    output-file: slides-session3-working-with-data.html
    theme: [default, dmgt.scss]
    logo: dmgt_asia_logo.png
    footer: "DMgtAsia · Introduction to R & RStudio"
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    slide-number: c/t
    show-slide-number: all
    progress: true
    transition: fade
    scrollable: true
    incremental: false
webr:
  packages:
    - dplyr
    - readr
engine: knitr
include-in-header:
  - text: |
      <script src="coi-serviceworker.js"></script>
other-links:
  - text: "View as slides"
    href: slides-session3-working-with-data.html
    icon: easel2
---

## What is Exploratory Data Analysis?

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}

- **EDA** is the critical first step.

- **EDA** is a state of mind.

- **EDA** is exploring your ideas.

- **EDA** has no strict rules.

- **EDA** helps understand your data.

- **EDA** is an iterative cycle.

- **EDA** is a creative process.

:::

::: {.column width="10%"}

:::

::: {.column width="30%"}

![](images/EDA.png)

:::
:::


## Definition of EDA

> *"detective work – numerical detective work – or counting detective work – or graphical detective  work"*

- **Tukey,  1977** *Page 1, Exploratory Data Analysis*



## How to do EDA?

- The easiest way to do **EDA** is to use questions as tools to guide your investigation.

- **EDA** is an important part of any data analysis, even if the questions are known already.



## Asking the right questions

Key to asking **_quality_** questions is to generate a large **_quantity_** of questions.

It is difficult to ask revealing questions at the start of the analysis.

But, each new question will expose a new aspect and increase your chance of making a discovery.

## What Questions to ask?


- What type of variation occurs within your variables?

- What type of covariation occurs between your variables?

- Whether your data meets your expectations or not.

- Whether the quality of your data is robust or not.


## Steps of a data analysis project {.smaller}


::: columns
::: {.column  width="40%"}

- Preparing Tidy Data
  - Data Cleaning
  - Data Wrangling

- Data Exploration
  - Transformation
  - Visualization

- Statistical Analysis
- Prepare Results
- Draw Inferences
- Report Findings

:::
::: {.column  width="60%"}
![](images/steps_SDA.png){width='60%' fig-align='center'}
:::
:::




## Data Wrangling in R

### `dplyr` Package

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}
The `dplyr` is a powerful R-package to manipulate, clean and summarize unstructured data.

In short, it makes data exploration and data manipulation easy and fast in R.
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}

![](images/dplyr.png)
:::

:::

## Verbs of the `dplyr`

- There are many verbs in `dplyr` that are useful.

![](images/dplyr_fns.JPG)


## Using the pipe operator (`|>` or `%>%`)

![](images/pipes_dplyr.png){fig-align="center" width='100%'}


## Let us load some data

The cells on this page run live in your browser. This first one loads the WHO TB data and runs
on its own (the first run takes a few seconds while `dplyr` loads).

```{webr}
#| autorun: true
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
options(width = 70, pillar.print_min = 5, pillar.print_max = 5)

download.file(
  "https://drarunmitra.github.io/DMgtAsia_DataScience_IntroR_site/data/who_tubercolosis_data.csv",
  "tb.csv"
)
tb <- read_csv("tb.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# used later, in the %in% demo
indian_subcont <- c("India", "Nepal", "Pakistan", "Bangladesh", "Afghanistan", "Sri Lanka")

tb
```

## Take a Quick Look at the data

`glimpse()` shows every column down the page. Press **Run**.

```{webr}
glimpse(tb)
```

## Check the first few rows

```{webr}
head(tb)
```

## Check the dimensions and the column names

```{webr}
dim(tb)

names(tb)
```

## Unique countries in the bottom 50 rows

With the pipe, the steps read left to right: take `tb`, keep the last 50 rows, list the
distinct countries.

```{webr}
tb |>
  tail(50) |>
  distinct(country)
```

## `distinct()` and `count()`

`distinct()` returns the distinct values of a column. `count()` returns each distinct value and
how many times it appears. Try both.

```{webr}
tb |>
  distinct(who_region)
```

```{webr}
tb |>
  count(who_region)
```

## `arrange()`

`arrange()` sorts by a column. Use `desc()` (or a leading `-`) to sort descending. Edit and
re-run.

```{webr}
tb |>
  count(who_region) |>
  arrange(n)
```

```{webr}
tb |>
  count(who_region) |>
  arrange(-n)        # alt: arrange(desc(n))
```

## Logical Operators in R

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

- To satisfy *all* of several conditions, use the "and" operator, `&`.

- The "or" operator `|` returns rows that meet *any* of the conditions.

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

![](images/LogicalOp.png)

:::

:::

## `filter()`: rows from 2015 on

```{webr}
tb |>
  filter(year >= 2015)
```

## `filter()`: all entries for India

```{webr}
tb |>
  filter(country == "India")
```

## Filter by year and country

```{webr}
tb |>
  filter(year >= 2015 & country == "India")
```

## The `%in%` operator

To filter a categorical variable for several levels at once, use `%in%`. `indian_subcont` was
defined in the first cell.

```{webr}
tb |>
  filter(country %in% indian_subcont) |>
  count(country)
```

## `summarize()`

`summarize()` collapses many values to one. `n()` counts rows; `n_distinct()` counts distinct
values.

```{webr}
tb |>
  summarize(
    n                = n(),
    n_countries      = n_distinct(country),
    n_years          = n_distinct(year),
    mean_hiv_percent = mean(hiv_percent, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_hiv_percent   = sd(hiv_percent, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
```

## `group_by()` and `summarize()`

The power of `summarize()` shows when you group first. Summaries are then computed per group.

```{webr}
tb |>
  group_by(who_region) |>
  summarize(
    n_countries = n_distinct(country),
    mean_inc    = mean(incidence_100k, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_inc      = sd(incidence_100k, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  ungroup()
```

## `mutate()` and `if_else()`

`mutate()` creates or changes a column. Here we flag the Indian subcontinent.

```{webr}
tb |>
  mutate(ind_sub = if_else(country %in% indian_subcont,
                           "Indian Subcontinent", "Others")) |>
  select(country, ind_sub) |>
  filter(ind_sub != "Others") |>
  distinct()
```

## Group by the new variable

```{webr}
tb |>
  mutate(ind_sub = if_else(country %in% indian_subcont,
                           "Indian Subcontinent", "Others")) |>
  group_by(ind_sub) |>
  summarize(
    n_countries = n_distinct(country),
    mean_inc    = mean(incidence_100k, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  ungroup()
```

## `case_when()`

Alternative to `if_else()` for more than two options.

![](images/case_when.jpg){fig-align="center"}

## `if_else()` vs `case_when()`

Use `if_else()` for two options. For more options, nested `if_else()` becomes hard to read, so
`case_when()` is clearer.

## `join()`

- Real projects combine several sources of data.

- The mutating joins combine data frames by matching rows on a key.

----

![](images/dplyr_joins.jpg)


## More on `join()`

- `left_join(x, y)` keeps all rows of `x`, adding matching columns from `y`.

- `right_join(x, y)` keeps all rows of `y`.

- `full_join(x, y)` keeps all rows from both.

- `inner_join(x, y)` keeps only rows present in both.


## Visual representation of the `left_join()`

![](images/dplyr_joins_left.png)


## `pivot()`

We often reshape data between long and wide format with the `pivot` family of functions.

![](images/long_wide.png){fig-align="center"}


## Other Useful Functions

- `drop_na()` removes rows with missing values in a chosen column.

- `select()` returns only the columns you name (the column counterpart of `filter()`).


## More Resources for `dplyr` {.smaller}

- The [Data Transformation cheatsheet](https://opensource.posit.co/resources/cheatsheets/data-transformation/) summarises the `dplyr` verbs on a single page.

- The [RStudio IDE cheatsheet](https://opensource.posit.co/resources/cheatsheets/rstudio-ide/) is a handy reference for the editor itself.

- Review the [Tibbles](https://r4ds.had.co.nz/tibbles.html) and [Transformations](https://r4ds.had.co.nz/transform.html) chapters of the free [**_R for Data Science_** book](http://r4ds.had.co.nz).

# Questions?

# Thank You!
